visual_checks: CRITICAL — TEXT LAYER VERIFICATION: Attempt to select text in the PDF. If no text can be selected on any page — or if selecting text grabs the entire page as a single image — the PDF is a scanned image without a text layer.
Look for telltale signs of scanned documents: slightly skewed text, speckled backgrounds, uneven lighting, visible scanner edges, or paper texture in the background.
Check whether the PDF has any searchable text by attempting a text search (Ctrl+F). If searching for a word visible on the page returns no results, the page is an image.
Examine the tag tree: if the entire document consists of one
Look for mixed content: some pages may be native text (from digital creation) while other pages are scanned images (appended from a scanner). Check every page, not just the first.
If OCR has been applied, check the quality: does the recognized text match what is visually displayed? Poor OCR produces garbled text that is worse than no text at all.
Check document properties for signs of scanning: creator applications like scanner software, page sizes matching standard scanner output, or uniform image-based pages.
pass_conditions: All text in the PDF is real, selectable, searchable text — not images of text
The document was created digitally (from Word, InDesign, web-to-PDF, etc.) and contains native text throughout
If the document was originally scanned, high-quality OCR has been applied and the recognized text accurately matches the visual content
Text search successfully finds words that are visible on every page of the document

The tag tree contains text-based tags (

,

-

, , etc.) corresponding to all visible text content
fail_conditions: The entire PDF consists of scanned page images with no text layer — text cannot be selected or searched
One or more pages are scanned images while others contain real text, and the scanned pages have no OCR applied
OCR has been applied but is of such poor quality that the recognized text does not match the visible content (garbled characters, missing words, incorrect readings)
The PDF contains images of text where the text could have been rendered as real text (e.g., a flyer created as a JPEG and inserted into a PDF instead of being exported as a text-based PDF)
The tag tree shows only <Figure> tags for pages that contain substantial text content, indicating the text is embedded in images
No text can be found via search despite pages full of visible text content
na_conditions: Document is not a PDF (DOCX, PPTX, and XLSX files inherently contain real text)
PDF contains only images, diagrams, or photos with no text content expected (e.g., a photo gallery PDF)
ne_conditions: AI response could not be parsed
PDF could not be opened or its text layer could not be inspected
The document is encrypted or restricted and text selection/search cannot be tested
common_mistakes: Scanning a signed paper document to PDF and posting it without running OCR — the resulting PDF is a flat image with no accessible text
Running OCR on a low-quality scan, producing inaccurate text recognition that gives screen readers garbled content (worse than no OCR in some cases)
Assuming a PDF has real text because it 'looks' like a normal document — scanned PDFs can look identical to native PDFs visually
Appending scanned pages (e.g., a signed signature page) to an otherwise text-based PDF without applying OCR to the scanned pages
Creating a document as an image (designing a flyer in Photoshop, saving as JPEG, inserting into PDF) instead of authoring it with real text in a word processor or design tool
Relying solely on automated OCR without reviewing the result — OCR engines make errors, especially with handwriting, low-resolution scans, or complex layouts

examples.pass: A PDF annual report was exported from InDesign. All 48 pages contain native, selectable, searchable text. The tag tree has full text-based tags. A screen reader can read every word on every page.
examples.fail: A 12-page PDF policy document was created by scanning printed pages on an office multifunction printer. The entire document consists of 12 full-page images. No text can be selected, no text search works, and a screen reader announces only 'graphic' on each page. The document is completely inaccessible.
examples.partial: A 20-page PDF contract has 18 pages of native text from the Word export, but pages 19 and 20 are scanned images of the signed signature pages with no OCR. A screen reader reads pages 1-18 normally but encounters only images on the last two pages.
